title: 🌋 Ring of Fire: Why 90% of Quakes Strike the Same Loop
description: The Ring of Fire is a 40,000-kilometer arc around the Pacific Ocean where about 90 percent of the world's earthquakes and 75 percent of its volcanoes occur. From Chile to Japan to New Zealand, the same plate tectonic geometry repeats. This single feature has shaped human history for thousands of years and continues to threaten more than 800 million people. #RingOfFire #PlateTectonics #Pacific #Earthquakes #DisasterDecode

segments.voice: Around the rim of the Pacific Ocean runs a 40,000-kilometer arc of seismic and volcanic activity. It begins on the southern coast of Chile, runs north through Peru, Ecuador, Colombia, Central America, Mexico, the United States, and Canada to Alaska. It crosses the Aleutian Islands, descends through Kamchatka, the Kuril Islands, Japan, the Philippines, and Indonesia. It continues south through Papua New Guinea, the Solomon Islands, Vanuatu, Fiji, Tonga, and finally reaches New Zealand. Approximately 90 percent of the world's earthquakes and 75 percent of its volcanoes occur within this single feature. It is called the Ring of Fire.
segments.visual: Pacific Ring of Fire 40000 kilometer arc Chile Japan New Zealand 90 percent earthquakes

segments.voice: The Ring of Fire is not a single tectonic feature but a collection of plate boundaries that happen to surround the Pacific Ocean. The Pacific Plate, the largest tectonic plate on Earth, is bordered on most sides by other plates moving toward it or away from it. Where the Pacific Plate is being pushed underneath another plate — a process called subduction — earthquakes and volcanoes are abundant. Where the Pacific Plate is sliding past other plates, fewer volcanoes form, but earthquakes still occur. The geometry produces consistent hazard around the entire Pacific basin.
segments.visual: tectonic plates Pacific Plate boundary subduction sliding consistent geometry basin

segments.voice: The Pacific Plate moves at about 7 to 11 centimeters per year. That motion drives the entire Ring of Fire. The forces involved are enormous. Modern measurements estimate that the kinetic energy stored in the Pacific Plate's movement, integrated over the timescales relevant to earthquakes, far exceeds any human-engineered system. The Ring of Fire releases this energy in pulses — earthquakes — separated by periods of strain accumulation. The total energy release per century is approximately equivalent to several hundred megatons of TNT.
segments.visual: Pacific Plate motion 7 to 11 centimeters per year kinetic energy strain pulses TNT

segments.voice: The deepest earthquakes on Earth occur within subducted slabs around the Ring of Fire. The Tonga subduction zone produces earthquakes at depths exceeding 700 kilometers. The Sea of Okhotsk earthquake of May 24, 2013, was a magnitude 8.3 event at 609 kilometers depth — one of the deepest large earthquakes ever recorded. These deep events are caused by minerals undergoing phase transitions at high pressure, releasing accumulated strain in ways that approximately mimic the failure of brittle rock.
segments.visual: deep earthquakes Tonga 700 kilometers Sea of Okhotsk 2013 8.3 phase transitions

segments.voice: The most lethal volcanoes on Earth also lie within the Ring of Fire. Krakatoa, Tambora, Pinatubo, Mount Pelée, Mount St. Helens, Mount Vesuvius — though Vesuvius itself is in the Mediterranean — all share the explosive style associated with subduction-zone volcanism. The chemistry that produces this style involves water dragged down with the subducted plate. The water reduces the melting temperature of the overlying mantle wedge, producing a sticky, gas-rich magma that erupts violently rather than as effusive lava flows.
segments.visual: lethal volcanoes Krakatoa Tambora Pinatubo Pelée St. Helens water mantle gas magma

segments.voice: The Andes mountain range owes its existence to the Ring of Fire. The Nazca Plate is being subducted beneath the South American Plate. The compression and uplift have raised the South American Cordillera over tens of millions of years. The same process produces earthquakes regularly along the Chilean and Peruvian coasts. The 1960 Valdivia earthquake in Chile, magnitude 9.5, remains the most powerful earthquake ever instrumentally recorded. The 2010 Maule earthquake, magnitude 8.8, was the sixth most powerful. Both occurred on the Andean subduction zone.
segments.visual: Andes Nazca Plate subduction uplift Cordillera Valdivia 1960 9.5 Maule 2010 8.8

segments.voice: Here's what nobody saw coming until plate tectonics was understood: the connection between Chile and New Zealand. Earthquakes thousands of kilometers apart along the Ring of Fire are connected by the same global geometry. The Cascadia subduction zone in Washington and Oregon is the geometric mirror image of the Chilean subduction zone. The Japan Trench is similar to the Aleutian Trench. The Tonga Trench is the deepest in the world, paired symmetrically with the Mariana Trench. The Ring is a single system with regional variations.
segments.visual: Ring connection Cascadia mirror Japan Aleutian Tonga Mariana global geometry system

segments.voice: The Ring of Fire's largest earthquakes occur on subduction zone megathrusts. These faults can extend for thousands of kilometers along strike and rupture in segments hundreds of kilometers long. The 2004 Sumatra-Andaman earthquake, magnitude 9.1, ruptured 1,300 kilometers of fault in about 10 minutes. The 2011 Tohoku earthquake, magnitude 9.1, ruptured about 500 kilometers in about 3 minutes. The 1964 Alaska earthquake, magnitude 9.2, ruptured about 800 kilometers in 5 minutes. Each released roughly the energy of millions of Hiroshima bombs.
segments.visual: megathrust rupture Sumatra 1300 kilometers Tohoku 500 Alaska 800 Hiroshima millions

segments.voice: Tsunamis are intrinsic to subduction zone earthquakes. When a megathrust ruptures, the seafloor above it can lift by several meters in seconds. The water column above is displaced. A tsunami radiates outward at speeds approaching 800 kilometers per hour in deep water. The 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami killed 230,000 people in 14 countries. The 2011 Japan tsunami killed about 18,500 people and triggered the Fukushima nuclear disaster. The 1960 Chile tsunami killed people as far away as Hilo, Hawaii, and Sanriku, Japan. Subduction zone tsunamis have global reach.
segments.visual: tsunami subduction seafloor lift 2004 230000 2011 Tohoku 1960 Chile Hilo Sanriku global

segments.voice: Approximately 800 million people live within reach of major Ring of Fire hazards. Tokyo, Yokohama, Osaka, Kobe, and Nagoya in Japan. Seoul in Korea. Manila in the Philippines. Jakarta in Indonesia. Auckland and Wellington in New Zealand. Lima, Santiago, Quito, and Bogotá in South America. Mexico City and Guatemala City in Central America. Los Angeles, San Francisco, Seattle, and Vancouver on the North American coast. The combined population of these urban areas is among the largest on Earth.
segments.visual: population 800 million Tokyo Manila Jakarta Lima Mexico Los Angeles Vancouver

segments.voice: Japan's seismic experience is the world's most documented. The Japan Meteorological Agency operates approximately 1,000 seismometers and a real-time tsunami warning system tested by major events. Japan's building codes are widely considered the world's most stringent for earthquake resistance. Schools in Japan run regular evacuation drills. Disaster preparedness is part of national education. The 2011 Tohoku event nonetheless caused approximately 20,000 deaths, mostly from tsunami inundation that exceeded engineered defenses.
segments.visual: Japan JMA 1000 seismometers tsunami warning building codes drills 2011 Tohoku 20000

segments.voice: Japan also produces about 10 percent of the world's annual seismic moment release. The country experiences perhaps 100,000 earthquakes per year, most too small to be felt. Approximately 1,500 are felt by people each year. About 15 to 20 cause some damage in any given year. The continuous low-level activity is part of normal Japanese life. The country's seismic monitoring data has formed the foundation for many international advances in earthquake science.
segments.visual: Japan 10 percent moment 100000 earthquakes 1500 felt 15 to 20 damage normal life

segments.voice: The Cascadia subduction zone in the Pacific Northwest is the least observed but among the most dangerous in the world. The fault has not produced a major earthquake in modern observational history. Paleoseismic evidence suggests it ruptured most recently on January 26, 1700, in a magnitude 9.0 event. The earthquake caused a tsunami documented in Japanese records as a wave arriving without local earthquake. Stronger evidence comes from coastal Cascadian sediment cores showing repeated subsidence and tsunami deposits roughly every 300 to 500 years.
segments.visual: Cascadia 1700 January 26 magnitude 9.0 Japan tsunami records sediment cores 300 to 500

segments.voice: The next Cascadia event could come tomorrow or in centuries. Probability of a magnitude 8 or larger earthquake on Cascadia in the next 50 years is estimated at approximately 15 percent. Probability of a magnitude 9 or larger is estimated at approximately 10 percent. The total population at risk includes Vancouver, Seattle, Portland, and many smaller communities. Building codes have been updated since 1980 to incorporate Cascadia hazard, but much of the existing housing predates these codes. Retrofitting is far behind what experts recommend.
segments.visual: Cascadia probability 15 percent 50 years Vancouver Seattle Portland codes retrofit gap

segments.voice: But the tremor was just the start of the volcanic side of the Ring. About 425 active volcanoes lie within the Ring of Fire's boundaries. Several erupt every year. Some erupt continuously. Mount Etna in Sicily, although strictly Mediterranean rather than Pacific, shares the subduction-zone style. Stromboli has erupted continuously for over 2,000 years. Sakurajima in Japan has had hundreds of small eruptions per year for decades. Soufrière in the Caribbean has been intermittently active since the 1990s.
segments.visual: Ring volcanoes 425 active Stromboli 2000 years Sakurajima continuous Soufrière

segments.voice: Mount St. Helens in Washington State erupted on May 18, 1980, after several months of warning. The eruption killed 57 people. The lateral blast obliterated 600 square kilometers of forest. The ash plume reached 24 kilometers high and spread across the United States. Mount St. Helens is now the most-monitored volcano on the U.S. mainland. The U.S. Geological Survey's Cascades Volcano Observatory in Vancouver, Washington, watches Mount St. Helens, Mount Rainier, Mount Hood, Mount Baker, and several others continuously.
segments.visual: St. Helens 1980 May 18 57 dead lateral blast 600 square kilometers USGS Cascades

segments.voice: Indonesian volcanoes are particularly active. Indonesia hosts approximately 130 active volcanoes — more than any other country. Mount Merapi in Java has had thousands of small eruptions and a few major ones. Mount Sinabung in Sumatra has been continuously active since 2010. Mount Agung on Bali erupted in 1963 with significant fatality and again in 2017 to 2019 without major casualties. The Pusat Vulkanologi dan Mitigasi Bencana Geologi monitors all 130 actively.
segments.visual: Indonesia 130 volcanoes Merapi Sinabung Agung 1963 2017 PVMBG monitoring

segments.voice: Earthquake early warning systems are one technology that has emerged from Ring of Fire experience. Japan's JMA system, operational since 2007, provides seconds of warning to phones, schools, factories, and trains before strong shaking arrives at distant locations. Mexico's SASMEX system has operated since 1993. ShakeAlert in California became operational in 2018. South Korea's Earthquake Early Warning system was deployed after the 2016 Gyeongju earthquake. Each system uses the difference in speed between fast P-waves and slow S-waves.
segments.visual: early warning JMA 2007 SASMEX 1993 ShakeAlert 2018 Korea P-wave S-wave

segments.voice: Tsunami warning systems are correspondingly mature. The Pacific Tsunami Warning Center in Honolulu has operated since 1949 and now serves as the international clearinghouse for Pacific tsunami threats. The Japan Meteorological Agency issues domestic warnings. The Indonesian government operates BMKG. Chile, Russia, and many Pacific island nations have their own systems. The DART buoy network operated by NOAA provides real-time deep-ocean confirmation of tsunami waves. The integrated system warns within minutes of major source events.
segments.visual: Pacific Tsunami Warning Center 1949 BMKG DART NOAA real-time minutes

segments.voice: What about preparedness for the rest of the population? Disaster education, drill exercises, and infrastructure resilience are uneven across the Ring of Fire. Japan and New Zealand are global leaders. Chile, after decades of disasters, has invested heavily in modern building codes. The United States and Canada are intermediate. Indonesia, the Philippines, and several Central American countries have improved substantially since the 2004 tsunami catalyzed regional cooperation. Other parts of the Ring remain less prepared.
segments.visual: preparedness uneven Japan New Zealand leaders Chile USA Canada Indonesia Philippines

segments.voice: The Ring of Fire's economic impact is substantial. The combined GDP of countries with significant Ring of Fire territory exceeds 30 trillion dollars annually. Major Ring of Fire disasters can cause economic losses of hundreds of billions per event. The 2011 Tohoku earthquake's economic impact, including the Fukushima cleanup, exceeded 200 billion dollars. The 1995 Kobe earthquake cost approximately 100 billion dollars. The cumulative annual cost of Ring of Fire disasters, averaged over decades, is in the tens of billions.
segments.visual: Ring economic GDP 30 trillion Tohoku 200 billion Kobe 100 billion annual tens

segments.voice: How long has the Ring of Fire existed? In its current configuration, approximately 200 to 250 million years. Plate tectonic reconstructions show that the Pacific Ocean opened during the Triassic and Jurassic periods, after the supercontinent Pangaea began breaking apart. The boundary between the Pacific Plate and surrounding plates has produced seismic and volcanic activity continuously since the Mesozoic. The Ring will continue to exist for as long as the Pacific Plate continues to subduct, which is geologically permanent on human timescales.
segments.visual: Ring age 200 to 250 million years Pacific opening Triassic Jurassic Pangaea Mesozoic

segments.voice: The Indian Ocean and the Atlantic Ocean both have less seismic and volcanic activity than the Pacific. The Indian Ocean has subduction zones — including the Sumatra and Java zones — but they are limited compared to the Pacific. The Atlantic Ocean is mostly bounded by passive margins; significant subduction occurs only in the Caribbean and around the South Sandwich Trench. The mid-Atlantic ridge produces frequent small earthquakes but few damaging ones at populated coastlines. The Pacific's geometry makes it uniquely active.
segments.visual: Indian Ocean Atlantic comparison passive margins Caribbean Sandwich mid-Atlantic ridge

segments.voice: Subscribe to DisasterDecode and hit the bell. Next week's disaster goes deeper into the Beirut port explosion of 2020 — when 2,750 tons of ammonium nitrate detonated in a Lebanese capital and reshaped a country.
segments.visual: Beirut port 2020 ammonium nitrate explosion Lebanon channel
